data.title: Northrop Grumman Says Defense Spending Could Reach Cold War Era Levels
data.description:
However, the company reaffirmed its FY26 adjusted EPS guidance and sales guidance, but it missed the consensus.

Earnings Snapshot

EPS of $6.14 exceeded the analyst consensus estimate of $6.09, and sales of $9.881 billion beat the street view of $9.752 billion.

Organic sales rose 5%, led by modernization efforts and strong demand in the defense sector.

Northrop disclosed total awards of $9.8 billion in the quarter and ended the period with a backlog of $96 billion.

The company reiterated the fiscal year 2026 sales outlook of $43.50 billion to $44.00 billion, against a $43.908 billion estimate. Also, it affirmed the adjusted EPS guidance of $27.40 to $27.90, versus the analyst estimate of $28.01.

Conference Call Takeaways

Northrop Grumman disclosed that it is expanding annual production capacity on the B-21 aircraft by 25%.

The company says that the outlook remains positive, with expected higher global defense spending and strong demand for missile defense systems and advanced interceptors.

NOC said the administration recently proposed a $1.5 trillion defense budget for fiscal year 2027, prioritizing modernization and reflecting a 44% increase over current spending levels.

The plan includes a $1.1 trillion base budget, roughly 30% higher than FY26.

It maintains funding for major programs such as B-21, Sentinel, IBCS, and E-2D, along with several classified projects.

If implemented, total defense spending would reach about 5% of GDP, up from around 3% in recent years and closer to Cold War-era levels.

For 2026, the company expects the growth to accelerate throughout the year in line with 2025 trends.

For the second quarter, NOC expects sales to grow in the high single digits sequentially, with broad-based growth across all segments for the full year.

Also, operating income is expected to remain strong, with margins in the low-to-mid 11% range and gradual improvement over the year driven by mix, timing, and execution.

The company now expects $1.85 billion in 2026 capex, including an additional $200 million to support B-21 production capacity.

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: Northrop Grumman stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $740.90. Recent analyst moves include:

  • Jefferies: Hold (Raises Target to $710.00) (April 9)
  • Citigroup: Buy (Raises Target to $807.00) (April 2)
  • Wells Fargo: Initiated with Overweight (Target $800.00) (April 1)

Top ETF Exposure

Significance: Because NOC carries significant weight in these funds, any significant inflows or outflows for these ETFs will likely force automatic buying or selling of the stock.

NOC Stock Price Activity: Northrop Grumman shares were up 0.80% at $616.00 during premarket trading on Wednesday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Photo via Shutterstock

data.title: Cramer Calls the Dip: Fear Is Lying—These Stocks Are Screaming Buy
data.description:
CNBC's Jim Cramer said recent sell-offs are creating buying opportunities in select stocks, backing his picks with a focus on fundamentals over fear-driven narratives.

CrowdStrike and Microsoft Highlight AI-Driven Resilience

CrowdStrike shares were under pressure following new AI-driven competition concerns.

The stock extended its decline after Anthropic launched Claude Code Security, an AI tool designed to detect and fix software vulnerabilities, raising fears about disruption in the cybersecurity space.

Recently, Bank of America Securities said Microsoft remains well-positioned for AI growth, with near-term stock performance tied to Azure and Copilot trends.

Analyst Tal Liani said Azure growth depends on how quickly Microsoft adds AI capacity, noting strong demand but limited infrastructure as a constraint, with further expansion expected to unlock upside.

He added that Copilot adoption is key to proving AI returns, as increased usage could drive higher revenue per user and ease competitive concerns.

Liani also pointed to steady growth in Microsoft's core businesses, including Microsoft 365, Dynamics, and LinkedIn, and maintained a Buy rating with a $500 price forecast.

Cramer's Thesis: Focus on Fundamentals, Not Fear

Cramer said investors should rely on "faith in management, faith in the model, faith in the balance sheet, faith in the comeback" when evaluating stocks during volatility.

While acknowledging that not every stock will recover, he said those who avoid panic selling often benefit, adding that investors who give in to fear may later wonder, "What were we thinking?"

Price Action: Microsoft shares were up 0.91% at $428.03, and CrowdStrike Holdings shares were up 1.26% at $455.26 during premarket trading on Wednesday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Photo via Shutterstock

data.title: Semiconductors Over Software: Why This Analyst Is Betting On Chips
data.description:
Analysts say strong AI-driven earnings are supporting tech stocks, but diverging trends between semiconductors and software are creating a more nuanced market outlook.

Earnings Strength and AI Demand Anchor Tech

Trivariate Research's Adam Parker told CNBC on Monday that robust earnings growth—especially in semiconductors—is underpinning the broader market.

Parker added that even if those forecasts moderate, earnings strength should help limit downside risk.

Similarly, Wedbush's Dan Ives said his Asia checks made him "incrementally more bullish," highlighting strong demand across chip supply chains and noting that the AI buildout shows no signs of slowing, with "no immediate supply risks."

Diverging Views on Semiconductors

Parker maintained a preference for semiconductors, calling them his "North Star" due to their earnings power and central role in the multi-year AI buildout, which he does not view as a bubble.

In contrast, BTIG's Jonathan Krinsky warned that semiconductor stocks look overheated after a strong run, noting they are trading about 17% above their 50-day moving averages and showing "too much cheer," which could lead to a near-term pullback.

Software Outlook Split Between Risk and Rebound

Parker said software is "structurally in trouble," citing high obsolescence risk, pressure on valuations, and sharper declines for companies that miss expectations.

However, Krinsky said software still has upside and continues to show momentum, while Ives pointed to a rebound in the sector, calling the earlier sell-off "the most disconnected" he has seen.

Image by Below the Sky via Shutterstock
